meta.title: 2.1 Props Are Arguments
meta.description: Components receive data the same way functions receive arguments.

blocks.prompt: In Level 1, you saw components like <MemberCard name="Alice" role="Engineer" />. Those name and role values had to get inside the MemberCard component somehow. Before reading this module, write down your best guess: how does a React component receive and use the data passed to it?

blocks.content:
## Props: how components receive data In Level 1, you learned that a React component is a function that returns JSX. Now we add one critical detail: that function can accept **arguments**. In React, these arguments are called **props** (short for "properties"). Here is the simplest example: ```jsx function Greeting({ name }) { return

Hello, {name}

; } ``` This component receives a prop called `name` and uses it in the JSX. When someone uses this component, they pass the name like this: ```jsx ``` The result on screen: **Hello, Sarah**. That is the entire mechanism. Props are how a parent component sends data to a child component. The parent decides what data to pass. The child decides how to display it. ### Destructuring: unpacking the props object Technically, React passes all props as a single object. The `{ name }` syntax in the function parameter is called **destructuring** -- it is JavaScript shorthand for "take the `name` property out of the object." These two versions are identical: ```jsx // Version 1: Destructuring (more common) function Greeting({ name }) { return

Hello, {name}

; } // Version 2: Using the props object directly function Greeting(props) { return

Hello, {props.name}

; } ``` In the wild, you will see both styles, but destructuring is far more common in modern React code. The advantage is readability -- you can see immediately what data the component needs by looking at the function parameter: ```jsx function UserCard({ name, email, role, avatarUrl }) { // You can immediately see this component needs // name, email, role, and avatarUrl return (

{name}

{email}

{role}
); } ``` The destructured parameter `{ name, email, role, avatarUrl }` is essentially a contract: "Give me these four pieces of data and I will render a user card." ### Passing different types of data Props are not limited to strings. Here is how different data types are passed: **Strings** use quotes: ```jsx } ); } ``` Reading the props: `type` defaults to "info" if not provided. `title` and `message` are required (no defaults). `dismissible` defaults to `true`. Reading the logic: the `icons` object maps types to emoji. The JSX uses the type to pick the right icon and CSS class. Reading the conditional: `{dismissible && }` means the Dismiss button only appears when `dismissible` is true. (You will learn more about this pattern in Module 2.3.) A parent might use this component like: ```jsx ``` Since `dismissible` is not passed, it defaults to `true`, and the Dismiss button appears. ### The mental model Think of props like filling out a form: - The **component definition** is the form template -- it lists what fields exist and which ones have default values - The **component usage** (the JSX tag with attributes) is the filled-out form -- it provides values for each field - The **rendered output** is the result of processing the filled-out form When you see a component and want to understand it, start with the function parameter to see what data it expects, then look at the JSX to see how it uses that data.

blocks.answer: It renders '100.00 EUR'. The amount prop is 100, currency is 'EUR' (overriding the default 'USD'), and showCents is not passed so it defaults to true. Since showCents is true, the amount is formatted with toFixed(2), giving '100.00'. The currency 'EUR' is appended after a space.

options: A) A 48x48 circular image loaded from /avatars/kim.jpg
B) A 32x32 circular image loaded from /avatars/Kim.jpg
C) A 48x48 square image loaded from /avatars/kim.jpg
problems.explanation: name is 'Kim' and size is 48 (explicitly passed). shape is not passed, so it defaults to 'circle'. The src uses a template literal that lowercases the name: /avatars/kim.jpg. Width and height are both set to 48 (the size prop). The className includes 'avatar-circle' because shape defaults to 'circle'.

options: A) severity, title, message, onClose, autoHide -- all are required
B) title, message, and onClose -- they have no default values
C) title and message -- onClose is optional because the button only shows when it is provided
problems.explanation: Props without a default value (no = sign) are effectively required: title, message, and onClose. severity defaults to 'info' and autoHide defaults to false, so they are optional. While onClose is technically optional in the sense that the component will not crash without it (the button just will not render), it has no default value in the destructuring, which means the component definition treats it as expected input.

options: A) Nothing is wrong -- this will work fine
B) amount is passed as a string '49.99' instead of a number {49.99}, so .toFixed(2) will crash
C) label and amount are in the wrong order
problems.explanation: When amount is written as amount="49.99", it is passed as the string '49.99', not the number 49.99. The component calls amount.toFixed(2), which is a number method -- calling it on a string causes an error. The fix is to pass it as amount={49.99} using curly braces, which passes it as a JavaScript number. Prop order does not matter in React.

blocks.prompt: Explain props in your own words. What is destructuring, and why is it useful for reading React code? How do default values work, and how can you tell which props are optional versus required?

blocks.content: Props are how parent components pass data to child components. Destructuring in the function parameter shows you exactly what data a component expects. Strings use quotes, everything else uses curly braces. Default values (with =) make props optional; props without defaults are effectively required.

blocks.prompt: Now you know how a single component receives props. But where do those prop values come from? The next module traces the flow: how a parent component's data becomes a child component's props, and what happens when props need to pass through multiple levels.

reviewQuestions.question: In React, what are props?
options: CSS styles applied to a component
Data passed from a parent component to a child component
JavaScript functions that run on page load
reviewQuestions.explanation: Props (short for properties) are how a parent component sends data to a child component -- like arguments to a function.
reviewQuestions.encourageCorrect: Nailed it! Props are the way data flows from parent to child in React.
reviewQuestions.encourageIncorrect: Not quite -- props are data passed from parent to child, like arguments to a function. The parent decides what to send, and the child decides how to display it.

reviewQuestions.question: What does a default value (like color = 'gray') in a prop destructuring mean?
options: The prop can never be changed
If the parent does not provide that prop, this fallback value is used
The prop is required and must always be 'gray'
reviewQuestions.explanation: A default value with = means 'use this if the parent does not pass anything for this prop.' It makes the prop optional.
reviewQuestions.encourageCorrect: That's right! Default values make props optional by providing a sensible fallback.
reviewQuestions.encourageIncorrect: Close! A default value like color = 'gray' means that if the parent doesn't provide a color prop, the component uses 'gray' as a fallback.

reviewQuestions.question: When passing a number as a prop, which syntax is correct?
reviewQuestions.explanation: Numbers (and all non-string values) are passed using curly braces. Quotes pass strings, so count="42" would be the string '42', not the number 42.
reviewQuestions.encourageCorrect: Perfect! Curly braces for numbers, quotes for strings -- you've got the pattern down.
reviewQuestions.encourageIncorrect: Remember: quotes are for strings, curly braces are for everything else. count={42} passes the number 42, while count="42" would pass the string '42'.
